title: So I gave a presentation at my University about Eva
display_title: So I gave a presentation at my University about Eva
wikitext:
[]: So I gave a presentation at my University about Eva ...             Quote Post by Armitage » Thu Jul 23, 2009 3:57 am Last semester (which was my senior semester) at my university , I gave a presentation titled "The Post-Modern Fantasy: Neon Genesis Evangelion In Context" . The main gist of my presentation was that when looking at Evangelion through the context of the other successful anime trends around it , one notices that it is an excellent mirror to some of the more radical post-modern ideas that are moving out there and gives it a form . My presentation was spread out into different sections spanning a whole hour or so of material (and then for those interested we watched the first Rebuild of Eva as a bonus although we did that at a private venue since the University wouldn't allow me to show it on campus after the presentation without paying like 700 dollar royalty fees or something). As the presentation was starting up and having all the people sit down , I began with having them listen to Kanon just to get them in the mood (plus university people tend to like feeling high brow) The first section dealt with the leadup to Evangelion in anime . I included some basics as the original Mobile Suite Gundam which helped to revolutionise the mecha genre which helped to set the stage for Evangelion . (I pointed out that Evangelion is by far not a mecha genre , but contains some of the mecha genre trappings that could only have been made possible through the development of the style etc etc) Secondly I pointed out the differences between popular Modernism which was an existential problem brought about by a giant world catastrophe (World War II) and the feeling of abandonment by God in a world that was suddenly devoid of reason ( This I also linked to Second Impact ) with the post-modern view that not only was the world devoid of meaning but now , through the advancement of technology , it would be MAN'S ability to become the new God and replace the God that abandoned it . This i dubbed the Apotheosis of Man . We see that in pre-rebuild Eva (I haven't seen the second Rebuild yet as I'm writing this) through Third Impact and Instrumentality where Teilhard de Chardin's Omega Point as well as other singularity-type ideas are assessed but can only be achieved through technological advancement (Unit 01 etc) . This fusion of man and machine i explored in the context of other anime such as Ghost in the Shell which , through its exploration of the cyberpunk ideas inherent in the internet and the combination of man and machine relates how the advancements in science since world war II have brought it as another avenue to solve the problem of Modernist existentialism (i.e. that God has abandoned the world) I also looked at other famous projects such as Princess Mononoke which pitted the old gods with technology as further evidence of the reaction towards the existential void by supplanting faith with science in the Japanese consciousness . I also placed these ideas in the historical context of Japan's failure in World War II , the precarious situation of Japan's spiritual unity , and its own existentialist questions that seemed to have been answered 'miraculously' through their technological advancement . However , I also pointed out the neurotic nature of Japanese society due to this rapid technological growth and linked that to the way Shinji reacts to the immense power of "recreating" his world . In other words , Anno indicates that Apotheosis alone without developing the soul is inherently empty and causes a great deal of neurotic behaviour and even psychosis . There were a few other tidbits in my presentation including the narrative structure of eastern entertain as opposed to western , but I wanted to hear what you guys thought of it so far .

[]:             Quote Post by xeon » Thu Jul 23, 2009 4:13 am What Major?

[]:             Quote Post by Armitage » Thu Jul 23, 2009 4:14 am xeon wrote: What Major?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jul 23, 2009 11:00 am Sounds like it was interesting, I know I would have enjoyed that presentation!

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jul 23, 2009 11:42 am I don't suppose you could offer a download link for a text copy, or powerpoint if you used it. I'd give it a look.

[]:             Quote Post by Axell » Thu Jul 23, 2009 11:48 am cool.

[]:             Quote Post by Armitage » Thu Jul 23, 2009 12:06 pm NemZ wrote: I don't suppose you could offer a download link for a text copy, or powerpoint if you used it. I'd give it a look.

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Thu Jul 23, 2009 12:18 pm Impressive.

[]:             Quote Post by schismatics » Thu Jul 23, 2009 12:34 pm Armitage wrote: Unfortunately , I lost it after my HD failed during early summer =( .

[]:             Quote Post by Gendo'sPapa » Thu Jul 23, 2009 4:08 pm Sounds fascinating. And promised to be a far more intelligent and worthy observation of Eva than that guy who prances around at conventions dressed like the dude from V For Vendetta . Hope everything went well.

[]:             Quote Post by Ramiel² » Thu Jul 23, 2009 7:00 pm Too bad you can`t upload the presäntation anymore,i`d like to see it. Seems you put really good effort in it. Btw Paradise Lost is a brilliant Quote for End of evangelion just wanna mention that!

[]:             Quote Post by Armitage » Thu Jul 23, 2009 7:58 pm Voideater: thanks : I was just happy I was able to do it at all . schismatics: I wish I had kept it , most of the anime-lovers at the presentation were impressed since it really put Evangelion into the kind of perspective that made it pertinent not just "because it's artsy" but because it reflects a trend in modern society that places it at the vanguard of musings of the Post-Modern and really solidifies Anno's statement that nothing has been newer than Evangelion even in the past 10 years because we have yet to move beyond Post-Modern philosophy and thought . In other words , the prophetic leaps of Evangelion's apotheotic conceptions of technology and man have yet to be realized or refuted nor has the need for the Japanese consciousness to once again find a spiritual direction overtaken the existential morass that has held sway since the end of World War II . After all , as I pointed out in my presentation , the Japanese , much like Europe , were horrendously traumatised by civilian deaths and atrocities (the Nuclear weapons for example) and have a clear emphasis on the consciousness of the population . I drew parallels with Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart and Wole Soyinka Death and the King's Horseman as premonitions for the kind of effect destructive outside forces can have on the worldview and religious convictions of a society or culture that considers failure or occupation as impossible . This trend of Modernist thought and connection with World War II culminates in Camus' The Plague and many of Sartre's work . By drawing parallels of the reaction of the West and Africa to massive destruction with the experience of Japan it was easy to see how the Apocalyptic nature of many of Japan's post-war anime and cinema take into account this shift to Modernism which eventually , through the miracle of industrial growth and the advent of internet , turn into the Post-modern culture we love/hate today . Gendo'sPapa: Thanks . Maybe it's because I grew old with Evangelion and had left anime in the backburner (though without growing out of my appreciation for it) that I was able to express this particular connection of Evangelion with the wider social consciousness growing in the world . Ramiel: Paradise Lost was an interesting choice . For those not familiar with the passage used in the trailer , it was the demon Beliel talking during the council in Hell of what to do . Should they seek another war with God , stay in Hell , or do something else (such as corrupt God's creation Man) . Beliel is explaining that to go up against God is to risk annihilation and that is an end that is far worse than hell (something most people in modern society might not completely understand) "For who would lose , though full of pain , this intellectual being" means who would want to stop having sentient existence even if that existence was hellishly painful . "those thoughts that wander through eternity" the idea of the ego , the I , the being itself . "to perish rather , swallowed up and lost in the wide womb of uncreated night" to return to the primordial nothingness that God created everything (a concept known as ex nihilo) The interesting connection between Milton and Evangelion can be seen in the idea of Choice as another post-modern emphasis : Will Shinji choose to cast out the world or to keep it , etc very much in tune with the question of whether the demons will avenge themselves in another vain attempt and also if Adam and Eve will Fall . Obviously the Adam and Eve connection is plentiful , but I was more concerned with the overt connection to support my post-modern thesis .

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 23, 2009 8:21 pm This is fascinating stuff, Armitage. I always feel that one aspect of NGE that escapes most in the West is its social context and the philosophical/political implications related to it. I think your placing of NGE in the post-modern context is dead-on and one could even draw parallels between its subversion of its anime tropes, the progressive theme of shedding away the persona/exterior to get to the existential individual dilemma underneath (which is also mirrored in the series' structure) and the post-modernist philosophical/social context you link it to. These kind of parallels are all over NGE and I often feel it's just a matter of getting people to recognize one aspect of it, of putting it into a new or little explored context to illuminate the overall concatenation between the 'in-series' aspects, its various formal concerns and all of these more 'outside' contexts. The relevant links seem endless and it's always fascinating to me when someone illuminates a new area how that seems to immediately link to others I was already aware of. Gendo'sPapa wrote: And promised to be a far more intelligent and worthy observation of Eva than that guy who prances around at conventions dressed like the dude from V For Vendetta .

[]:             Quote Post by TheWaffleCult » Thu Jul 23, 2009 8:59 pm Fascinating stuff Armitage! Wish I could have seen the presentation. I worked Evangelion into a paper/presentation I gave earlier this year for film studies, but mine was a bit less deep - my work had to do with American and Japanese cartoons linked with their respective cultures.

[]:             Quote Post by schismatics » Thu Jul 23, 2009 9:43 pm Armitage wrote: PURE AWESOMENESS
